author: @MsZeeZed
text:
A University education is a concentrated orientation of what everyone else has discovered and yes in the hard sciences this used to be crushing. 

The methodology changed dramatically in the C21st with something called “Problem Based Learning” whereby students are given a few lectures on the basics and then in small groups are presented with a series of practical problems to resolve in the way they think best and then as a wider group to openly discuss what they did and why. As a support worker for a University this methodology required a transformation in the structure of the teaching spaces themselves and lateral thinking on the technology used. These changes in the methodology of “how to teach” are more radical than anything most of us educated in the C20th will have experienced, were having an iron wrist to take handwritten notes with a pen for 30 hours a week was the essential criteria 😹

author: @Palmit_
text: NIKE (just do it). sure, use the skills learned, but NOT the mindset taught. once you've those skills in your pocket.. they are yours to experiment with.  there are no boundary's because they are yours. nothing but own self can decide limits of what to try.  heck, repeat old experiments, just to satiate yourself and truely know. reading about things somebody else did is by no means conclusive. Do things that are insane and 'could never work'.. thats how you prove to yourself and at same time, open up opportunities and potentials. good luck fellow inventors. :)

author: @HansHartman
text:
Robert,
So true, Dear Sir! I have many friends who have gone on to higher education and gotten advanced degrees. Only one of them have a patent to their name. And that patent is shared. I on the other hand have a patent for a product (an ice cream scoop) that will take that market by storm. If I sell just enough, it will give me the means to go after many other patents I am pursuing. The greatest is one that will “teach the blind to see again.”
In the words of Willie Wonka, 10% inspiration and 99% perspiration. 
If you don’t work at it, it doesn’t matter what degree you have. I know the Wright Brothers didn’t have a degree in Aeronautical Engineering.

author: @beamer.electronics
text: I mostly agree, but I can testify that if you can't explain your device (even though it works and they like it), in a high level of maths and physics -  significant aerospace and automotive companies sadly won't take your idea/product! You will be viewed cynically and with suspicion, and if your idea is genuinely desirable - they'll do it themselves or engage an established contractor. However, this advice is about 30 years old, and I sincerely hope things have changed.

author: @ClownWhisper
text: The only advantage a University degree gives you is a piece of paper and some extra tools for your toolbox. Now anybody can gain those tools on the fly mainly on the Internet or a library by a little research and you only need to learn the ones you need to learn on the fly. You can have a complete set of tools for the project that you are trying to accomplish with very little effort. Nobody is going to take the entire body of knowledge of a degree in electronic engineering and apply it to some small Discovery project it's not going to require that and nobody would attempt to utilize all their tools in that toolbox it's just not necessary. If you have the will and The thirst and the drive to find out what you need to know to accomplish the limited scope of your project you will do that without hardly even trying and you will do that as well as anybody with a piece of paper

author: @DerekWoolverton
text: You also have to accept the fact that you may not ever get something to go.  I have friends that are brilliant and come up with wonderful ideas, but they are years ahead of the market, and after a bit they lose interest and move on to another idea.  If you want to be rich, become a dentist.  If you want to spend a lifetime pursuing interesting ideas, accept that the journey may be your only reward.

author: @scotttovey
text:
It's kind of like the drought issue in the western US.

They've been predicting it for 30 years but no one was willing to pay for the solution of building enough desalination plants. Technology that already existed.

Now there's a bunch of people complaining about the fact that farmers have to use an enormous amount of water to grow food that they need to eat in order to survive.

We all have an idiot side that if we are not careful will get loose and take over our thinking, our responses and our actions. Keeping this in mind is what keeps us humble.


Why do some people think they are smarter and therefore superior than others?
It's quite simple.
They aren't smart enough to recognize that their idiot side is in charge.

author: @michaelwarbon
text: Hi Rob.  Here's my craziest idea I am mulling - it's a little tedious but bear with me.  Biogas is cheap to make compared to other sources.  A C4 plant like 🌵 captures carbon 20 times faster than trees.  And it turns out it can be easily turned into Biomethane.  It can be grown year round cheap with only red LED lights.  But this is not all..I would then convert a percentage (10 to 20%) of the methane into Hydrogen and Carbon (CNT or graphene) using pyrolysis wirh a  bi metal catalyst for CNT or copper (using your copper coating approach) on an extremely high surface area to make with a sonicator to make a constant flow of graphene.   If this can be done economically which I think it can, then the Hydrogen would then dope the remaining Biomethane to make something like Hythane (15% Hydrogen and 85% methane).  Typical cars can be economically (a couple to a few thousand cost) converted to run on this methane/hydrogen blend.  In terms of impact of atomspheric CO2, this would reverse it if the Hydrogen is generated with the pyrolysis process and if the neutral biogas is used (which would just already use CO2 present in the air).  Economically it is doable and one is not fighting against the superiority of hydrocarbons as a fuel source.

author: @ricksanchez3176
text:
Hi Rob, 
I come from a background of putting a lot of ugly red marks, (as built notes) on pretty blueprints. Push to shove, the engineers worked for me, fortunately, I could hardly imagine a better group of guys to work with, so very much a team effort, and no egos coming down. Smart people looking to fix mistakes/problems not compound or hide them.

      You're absolutely right. To a point when it comes to innovation, sometimes "knowing" will not work can be detrimental.  I've seen brilliant solutions to a problem from very unlikely sources. When I was pretty young I would be dragged around jobsites just to answer  questions related to my craft
 Every now and again I'd add an idea without being asked, something for a problem out of my field.  Now the guys that knew me, would look straight at me, but there were a few that would kind of kick their toes, and look like "why is this guy even talking?"  That changed real quick. Most of the people I worked for, had trade courses and job experience transferred to credits, a couple correspondence courses and 6 months class time they would get bachelor degrees at 40-45 years old.  They did, before they had the credentials saying they could.

**I digressed from the actual point. 😀. At the site meetings, as with most "kids" I'd put out my ideas very softly, finally guys who were actually looking out for me would say "just spit it out". Be confident, someone doesn't know what they got until it is presented and tried. May not be on inventing, but finding solutions, which is the mother of it.

author: @MrXaeox
text: One of the biggest problems with science that does seem to be changing in recent years is that scientists for a long time were not writing papers that didn't have a predicted or otherwise beneficial outcome. However as Newton said, "If I have seen further, it is by standing on the shoulders of giants". Basically I think trial and error will lead to failures, but documented failures can be invaluable in their own right.
